case_name: Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India
facts: The validity of the Aadhaar biometric identification card was challenged on the ground that it violated the citizen's fundamental right to privacy, which was not explicitly mentioned in the Constitution.
legal_issue: Whether the Right to Privacy is a fundamental right under the Indian Constitution, and whether it forms an intrinsic part of Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty).
judgment: A nine-judge bench unanimously held that the right to privacy is protected as an intrinsic part of the right to life and personal liberty under Article 21 and as a part of the freedoms guaranteed by Part III of the Constitution. It established the triple test for state intrusion: legality, legitimate state aim, and proportionality.
ratio_decidendi: The right to privacy is an element of human dignity and personal liberty under Article 21. Privacy is not lost in public spaces, and any restriction must be legal, necessary, and proportionate.
legal_principle: Right to Privacy is a fundamental right. State actions encroaching on privacy must satisfy the test of legality, necessity, and proportionality.

case_name: Jilubhai Nanbhai Khachar v. State of Gujarat
facts: Land owners challenged the constitutional validity of the land acquisition act and amendments which allowed state acquisition of land for public projects, claiming it deprived them of their property without adequate compensation and violated Article 300A.
legal_issue: Whether deprivation of property under Article 300A requires the state to pay market value compensation, and whether right to property forms part of basic structure.
judgment: The Supreme Court held that the right to property under Article 300A is not a basic structure of the Constitution. The state can acquire land by law for public purpose, and the compensation need not be market value as long as it is not illusory.
ratio_decidendi: The right to property is a constitutional right under Article 300A, not a fundamental right. Deprivation of property must be by law and for a public purpose, and the court will not review adequacy of compensation unless it is arbitrary or illusory.
legal_principle: Right to property is a constitutional right under Article 300A. State can acquire property for public purpose by law, and compensation need not be market rate.

case_name: Shreya Singhal v. Union of India
facts: Section 66A of the Information Technology Act criminalized sending offensive messages through a computer resource or other communication devices, leading to the arbitrary arrest of individuals criticizing politicians online.
legal_issue: Whether Section 66A of the IT Act is unconstitutional as violating the freedom of speech and expression guaranteed under Article 19(1)(a), and whether it falls under the exceptions in Article 19(2).
judgment: The Supreme Court struck down Section 66A of the IT Act in its entirety, holding that it was vague, overbroad, and had a chilling effect on free speech. The restrictions did not fall under any exceptions specified in Article 19(2), such as public order or clear incitement.
ratio_decidendi: Vague and overbroad laws criminalizing speech violate Article 19(1)(a). The clear line between discussion, advocacy, and incitement must be maintained; only incitement can be restricted under Article 19(2).
legal_principle: Free speech online cannot be curtailed using vague or overbroad laws. Restrictions must be narrowly tailored and strictly fall under the categories in Article 19(2).

case_name: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
facts: The passport of Maneka Gandhi was impounded by the government 'in public interest' without providing any reasons or opportunity of being heard, prompting a challenge under fundamental rights.
legal_issue: Whether 'procedure established by law' under Article 21 means any procedure enacted by a legislature or if it must be fair, just, and reasonable, and whether Articles 14, 19, and 21 are mutually exclusive.
judgment: The Supreme Court held that the procedure depriving a person of life or personal liberty under Article 21 must be fair, just, and reasonable, not arbitrary or oppressive. It ruled that Articles 14, 19, and 21 form a 'golden triangle' and must be read together, introducing substantive due process.
ratio_decidendi: A procedure under Article 21 must be just, fair, and reasonable, incorporating the principles of natural justice. Fundamental rights are not mutually exclusive silo compartments.
legal_principle: The 'procedure established by law' under Article 21 must be just, fair, and reasonable. Articles 14, 19, and 21 are interconnected.

case_name: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
facts: A Hindu mutt head challenged the Kerala government's land reform acts which sought to restrict the mutt's management of property, questioning the power of Parliament to amend fundamental rights.
legal_issue: What is the scope of Parliament's power to amend the Constitution under Article 368, and can it amend the fundamental rights?
judgment: A 13-judge bench, in a 7:6 majority, established the 'Basic Structure Doctrine'. It held that while Parliament has wide powers to amend the Constitution under Article 368, it cannot alter or destroy its basic structure (e.g., secularism, democracy, federalism, judicial review).
ratio_decidendi: Parliament's amending power under Article 368 is not unlimited. The constituent power does not include the power to damage, emasculate, or destroy the basic features or core identity of the Constitution.
legal_principle: Parliament cannot amend the core features or 'basic structure' of the Constitution under Article 368.

case_name: Union of India v. K.A. Najeeb
facts: Najeeb was accused under the Unlawful Activities Prevention Act (UAPA) and remained in jail as an undertrial for over 5 years. He applied for bail on the ground that his right to a speedy trial was violated.
legal_issue: Whether statutory restrictions on bail under UAPA can override the Supreme Court's constitutional powers to grant bail for violation of the right to a speedy trial under Article 21.
judgment: The Supreme Court held that the constitutional court's power to grant bail to protect the right to a speedy trial under Article 21 overrides statutory restrictions on bail found in special acts like UAPA. Prolonged incarceration without trial violates Article 21.
ratio_decidendi: Statutory restrictions on bail in special laws (e.g. Section 43D(5) of UAPA) cannot restrict the jurisdiction of constitutional courts to enforce fundamental rights like speedy trial and personal liberty under Article 21.
legal_principle: Statutory restrictions on bail cannot oust the constitutional court's duty to protect personal liberty and speedy trial under Article 21.

case_name: Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India
facts: Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) criminalized 'carnal intercourse against the order of nature' between consenting adults, which was challenged as violating sexual autonomy.
legal_issue: Whether Section 377 of the IPC is unconstitutional in so far as it criminalizes consensual sexual acts between adults of the same sex.
judgment: The Supreme Court decriminalized consensual homosexual acts between adults, striking down Section 377 IPC to that extent. It held that the provision violated the rights to equality (Article 14), non-discrimination (Article 15), expression (Article 19), and privacy and dignity (Article 21).
ratio_decidendi: Section 377 IPC, in so far as it criminalized consensual sexual acts between adults in private, was arbitrary and discriminatory. Discrimination based on sexual orientation violates Article 15, and consensual intimacy is protected under Article 21 privacy.
legal_principle: Consensual sexual activity between adults in private is protected under Article 21 privacy. Section 377 IPC violated the golden triangle.

case_name: D.K. Basu v. State of West Bengal
facts: A petition was filed raising concern about the increasing number of custodial violence and deaths in police custody, calling for judicial guidelines.
legal_issue: What safeguards must be put in place to prevent custodial torture and deaths, and how should police power during arrest be regulated?
judgment: The Supreme Court issued 11 mandatory guidelines (DK Basu guidelines) for police officers during arrest and detention, including wearing name tags, preparing arrest memos, informing relatives, and medical checkups.
ratio_decidendi: Custodial violence and torture violate the right to life under Article 21. The state is liable for constitutional wrongs and must pay compensation for rights violations.
legal_principle: Custodial torture violates Article 21. Specific procedural safeguards must be followed during arrest and detention to protect personal liberty.

case_name: Arnesh Kumar v. State of Bihar
facts: The petitioner filed for anticipatory bail apprehending arrest in a dowry harassment case under Section 498A IPC, leading the court to address routine and arbitrary police arrests.
legal_issue: Can the police automatically arrest an accused upon registration of an offense under Section 498A IPC, and how should Section 41 CrPC arrest powers be regulated?
judgment: The Supreme Court held that arrests should not be routine or automatic. It directed police officers to follow the checks under Section 41 CrPC and provide reasons to the magistrate before seeking detention.
ratio_decidendi: Arrest should only be made when there is a reasonable belief of commission and a necessity to arrest (e.g. to prevent absconding or tampering). Automotic arrest violates Articles 21 and 22.
legal_principle: Routine or mechanical arrests by the police violate the right to personal liberty. Arrest must satisfy the conditions of Section 41 CrPC.

case_name: Indra Sawhney v. Union of India
facts: The government implemented the Mandal Commission report recommending 27% reservation for Other Backward Classes (OBCs) in public employment, which was challenged in court.
legal_issue: Whether reservation can be based solely on economic criteria, whether it can exceed 50%, and whether backward classes can be identified by caste.
judgment: The Supreme Court upheld the 27% reservation for OBCs, but introduced the concept of the 'creamy layer' to exclude affluent backward class members. It held that reservation cannot exceed a 50% limit unless there are extraordinary circumstances, and backward classes can be identified by caste.
ratio_decidendi: Backward classes can be identified on the basis of caste. Reservation in employment is limited to a 50% ceiling, and economic criteria alone cannot justify reservations without social backwardness.
legal_principle: Reservations in public employment are limited to a 50% ceiling. The 'creamy layer' must be excluded from OBC reservations.

case_name: M. Nagaraj v. Union of India
facts: Constitutional amendments enabling reservations in promotions and consequential seniority for SC/ST employees were challenged as violating the basic structure of equality.
legal_issue: Whether amendments enabling reservation in promotions violate Article 14/16 or the basic structure, and what conditions must the state meet to implement promotions quotas.
judgment: The Supreme Court upheld the constitutional validity of the amendments but laid down three controlling conditions: the state must show backwardness, inadequacy of representation, and efficiency of administration (Article 335) backed by quantifiable data.
ratio_decidendi: Quotas in promotions are valid if the state provides quantifiable data showing backwardness of the class, inadequacy of their representation, and that overall administrative efficiency is maintained.
legal_principle: Reservations in promotions for SC/STs are constitutionally valid but require the state to produce quantifiable data on backwardness and inadequacy.

case_name: S.R. Bommai v. Union of India
facts: Several state governments led by opposition parties were dismissed by the President under Article 356 (President's Rule) on questionable grounds, including secularism violations.
legal_issue: Whether the President's proclamation under Article 356 is subject to judicial review, and what constitutes a 'failure of constitutional machinery'.
judgment: The Supreme Court held that the President's power under Article 356 is not absolute and is subject to judicial review. The court can examine whether material existed to justify President's Rule. Secularism was declared a basic feature, and the floor test was made mandatory to prove majority.
ratio_decidendi: The power under Article 356 is conditional. The proclamation is subject to judicial review, and assembly dissolution can only happen after Parliamentary approval. Secularism is part of the basic structure.
legal_principle: President's Rule under Article 356 is subject to judicial review. Secularism is part of the basic structure. The floor test is the only valid way to determine a government's majority.

case_name: Mohinder Singh Gill v. Chief Election Commissioner
facts: During parliamentary elections, the Chief Election Commissioner cancelled the poll in a constituency due to violence and ballot box tampering, which was challenged.
legal_issue: What is the scope of the Election Commission's powers under Article 324, and can it cancel an election and order a repoll?
judgment: The Supreme Court held that Article 324 is a plenary provision vesting comprehensive power in the Election Commission to conduct free and fair elections, including the power to cancel polls and order repolls when necessary, filling legislative gaps.
ratio_decidendi: Article 324 is a reservoir of power for the Election Commission. When there is no specific legislation to cover a situation, the commission has the inherent power to act in order to maintain the integrity of elections.
legal_principle: The Election Commission has wide, independent powers under Article 324 to ensure the integrity of the electoral process.

case_name: A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras
facts: A communist leader challenged his preventive detention under the Preventive Detention Act, 1950.
legal_issue: Whether preventive detention violates Article 19, and whether 'procedure established by law' means due process.
judgment: The Supreme Court held that 'procedure established by law' refers to lex (statute law) and not jus (natural law), and rejected the due process argument. This narrow reading was later overruled in Maneka Gandhi.
ratio_decidendi: Article 21 does not incorporate the American concept of procedural due process. It only requires a validly enacted law.
legal_principle: Procedural due process is not part of Article 21; legislative procedure is sufficient.

case_name: State of Madras v. Champakam Dorairajan
facts: The Madras government issued a Communal G.O. reserving seats in medical and engineering colleges based on caste proportions, which was challenged by a candidate.
legal_issue: Whether reservations based on caste in educational admissions violate Article 29(2), and whether Directive Principles override Fundamental Rights.
judgment: The Supreme Court struck down the communal quota, holding that Directive Principles cannot override Fundamental Rights. This judgment led to the First Constitutional Amendment inserting Article 15(4).
ratio_decidendi: Fundamental Rights are sacrosanct and cannot be abridged by Directive Principles. Reservation in admissions violates non-discrimination principles.
legal_principle: Fundamental Rights override Directive Principles. Caste-based educational quotas violate non-discrimination under Article 29(2).

case_name: Romesh Thappar v. State of Madras
facts: A ban on the entry and circulation of a weekly journal 'Cross Roads' in Madras was challenged by its editor.
legal_issue: Whether the ban on circulation violates the freedom of speech and expression, and whether 'public safety' falls within the exceptions in Article 19(2).
judgment: The Supreme Court struck down the ban, holding that freedom of speech includes the freedom of circulation. It ruled that 'public safety' was not a ground for restriction under Article 19(2) as it existed then (which led to the First Amendment adding 'public order').
ratio_decidendi: Freedom of speech and expression includes the freedom of propagation of ideas, which is ensured by freedom of circulation.
legal_principle: Freedom of speech includes the right to circulate. Restrictions must strictly fall under Article 19(2) exceptions.

case_name: Shankari Prasad v. Union of India
facts: The validity of the First Constitutional Amendment Act, 1951, which restricted property rights and introduced the Ninth Schedule, was challenged.
legal_issue: Whether an amendment under Article 368 is a 'law' under Article 13(2), and can Parliament amend Fundamental Rights.
judgment: The Supreme Court upheld the amendment, ruling that the power to amend the Constitution under Article 368 is constituent and not legislative, so it does not fall under 'law' in Article 13(2).
ratio_decidendi: Amendments under Article 368 are constituent acts and are not 'law' within the meaning of Article 13(2). Parliament has power to amend any part of the Constitution.
legal_principle: Parliament has the power to amend Fundamental Rights. Constitutional amendments are not 'laws' under Article 13.

case_name: Sajjan Singh v. State of Rajasthan
facts: The validity of the 17th Constitutional Amendment, which put land reform laws in the Ninth Schedule, was challenged.
legal_issue: Whether Parliament's power to amend the Constitution includes abridging fundamental rights, and whether Article 368 amendments require state ratification.
judgment: The Supreme Court, by a 3:2 majority, upheld the amendment, following Shankari Prasad. However, Justice Hidayatullah and Justice Mudholkar expressed doubts, sowing seeds for Golaknath.
ratio_decidendi: The constituent power under Article 368 is distinct from ordinary legislative power and includes the power to amend any provision of the Constitution, including Part III.
legal_principle: Reaffirmed Parliament's absolute power to amend the Constitution, including fundamental rights.

case_name: Golaknath v. State of Punjab
facts: A petition challenged the validity of land ceiling acts and the 17th Amendment putting them in the Ninth Schedule.
legal_issue: Whether Parliament can amend Fundamental Rights, and whether an amendment is 'law' under Article 13.
judgment: An 11-judge bench, by a 6:5 majority, held that Parliament cannot amend Fundamental Rights to take them away. It ruled that an amendment is a 'law' under Article 13(2) and subject to Part III. The court applied prospective overruling.
ratio_decidendi: Constitutional amendments are 'law' under Article 13(2). Fundamental rights are transcendental and cannot be abridged by Parliament.
legal_principle: Parliament has no power to amend Fundamental Rights. Amendments are subject to Article 13 scrutiny.

case_name: Indira Nehru Gandhi v. Raj Narain
facts: Raj Narain challenged the election of PM Indira Gandhi. During the appeal, Parliament passed the 39th Amendment inserting Article 329A, which took the PM's election out of judicial purview.
legal_issue: Whether the 39th Constitutional Amendment (Article 329A) violates the basic structure of the Constitution.
judgment: The Supreme Court struck down Article 329A(4) as unconstitutional, holding that free and fair elections, rule of law, and judicial review are parts of the basic structure, which cannot be destroyed.
ratio_decidendi: Free and fair elections and judicial review are basic features. Excluding judicial resolution of election disputes violates the basic structure.
legal_principle: Free and fair elections, rule of law, and judicial review are part of the basic structure. The 39th Amendment is invalid.

case_name: ADM Jabalpur v. Shivkant Shukla
facts: During the 1975 Emergency, several political leaders were detained under MISA. They filed habeas corpus petitions in High Courts, which were challenged by the state.
legal_issue: Whether a habeas corpus petition is maintainable during a presidential order suspending the right to enforce Article 21.
judgment: A 5-judge bench, by a 4:1 majority (with Khanna dissenting), held that no person has locus standi to file habeas corpus during emergency if Article 21 enforcement is suspended.
ratio_decidendi: Article 21 is the sole repository of the right to life and liberty. If its enforcement is suspended under Article 359, no court can inquire into detention validity.
legal_principle: Emergency presidential orders suspend the right to file habeas corpus. (Khanna dissented, holding right to life is natural and exists independently).

case_name: Minerva Mills v. Union of India
facts: The nationalization of Minerva Mills under a textile act was challenged, questioning the 42nd Amendment which sought to give Directive Principles absolute primacy over Fundamental Rights and bar judicial review.
legal_issue: Whether clauses of the 42nd Amendment removing limits on amending power and abridging judicial review violate the basic structure.
judgment: The Supreme Court struck down Sections 4 and 55 of the 42nd Amendment. It held that judicial review and the limited amending power of Parliament are basic features, and a harmony between FRs and DPSPs is part of the basic structure.
ratio_decidendi: The limited amending power of Parliament is itself a basic feature. Parliament cannot expand its amending power into an absolute power. Harmony between FRs and DPSPs is essential.
legal_principle: Parliament cannot use its amending power to destroy basic features or bar judicial review. Harmony between FRs and DPSPs is basic structure.

case_name: Waman Rao v. Union of India
facts: Validity of agricultural land ceiling acts and the Ninth Schedule was challenged.
legal_issue: Whether acts placed in the Ninth Schedule after the Kesavananda judgment (April 24, 1973) are open to challenge on fundamental rights.
judgment: The Supreme Court held that all amendments to the Constitution and acts added to the Ninth Schedule after April 24, 1973, are subject to judicial review on basic structure grounds.
ratio_decidendi: The basic structure doctrine applies to all constitutional amendments made after April 24, 1973. Acts added to the Ninth Schedule post-Kesavananda must not destroy the basic structure.
legal_principle: Acts placed in the Ninth Schedule after April 24, 1973, are open to challenge if they violate the basic structure.

case_name: S.P. Gupta v. Union of India
facts: A group of lawyers filed petitions challenging the transfer and non-appointment of High Court judges, raising questions on judicial independence.
legal_issue: Whether 'consultation' under Article 124 means 'concurrence', and who has primacy in judicial appointments (Executive or Judiciary).
judgment: The Supreme Court (First Judges Case) held that 'consultation' does not mean 'concurrence' and that the executive has primacy. However, it also recognized the standing of public-spirited citizens to file petitions, establishing PIL.
ratio_decidendi: Consultation does not mean concurrence. Independence of the judiciary is a basic feature, but executive has ultimate authority in appointments. Locus standi is relaxed for public interest.
legal_principle: Executive has primacy in judicial appointments (overruled in Second Judges Case). Established the concept of Public Interest Litigation (PIL).

case_name: Olga Tellis v. Bombay Municipal Corp.
facts: Bombay Municipal Corporation decided to evict pavement and slum dwellers from Bombay without offering alternative accommodation.
legal_issue: Whether eviction of pavement dwellers without notice and rehabilitation violates the right to life under Article 21 and right to equality.
judgment: The Supreme Court held that the right to life under Article 21 includes the right to livelihood. Pavement dwellers cannot be evicted without a fair procedure (prior notice and hearing) and alternative accommodation.
ratio_decidendi: The right to livelihood is an integral part of the right to life under Article 21. Evictions must follow natural justice and procedural fairness.
legal_principle: The right to life under Article 21 includes the right to livelihood. Eviction of pavement dwellers requires natural justice.

case_name: M.C. Mehta v. Union of India
facts: Oleum gas leaked from a Shriram Food and Fertilizer plant in Delhi, causing one death and several injuries, leading to a PIL on hazardous industries.
legal_issue: Whether a private enterprise engaged in hazardous activity is strictly liable for damage, and what is the scope of Article 32 writ powers in compensation.
judgment: The Supreme Court established the Doctrine of Absolute Liability, which holds that an enterprise engaged in inherently dangerous activity is absolutely liable for escape, allowing no exceptions (unlike strict liability).
ratio_decidendi: Enterprises engaged in hazardous activities owe an absolute and non-delegable duty to the community. Liability is absolute and not subject to strict liability exceptions (e.g. act of God).
legal_principle: Established the Doctrine of Absolute Liability for hazardous industries. Expanded PIL and compensation power under Article 32.

case_name: L. Chandra Kumar v. Union of India
facts: Constitutional provisions (inserted by 42nd Amendment) establishing administrative tribunals and excluding the jurisdiction of High Courts and Supreme Court were challenged.
legal_issue: Whether excluding the writ jurisdiction of High Courts (Article 226) and Supreme Court (Article 32) violates the basic structure.
judgment: The Supreme Court struck down the provisions excluding judicial review. It held that the power of judicial review vested in High Courts and Supreme Court is an essential and integral part of the basic structure.
ratio_decidendi: Judicial review under Articles 226 and 32 is part of the basic structure of the Constitution. Tribunals can act as supplemental bodies but cannot exclude High Court jurisdiction.
legal_principle: Judicial review is part of the basic structure. The jurisdiction of High Courts under Article 226 cannot be excluded by tribunals.

case_name: Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan
facts: A social worker in Rajasthan was gang-raped for opposing child marriage. Petitions were filed seeking safe working conditions for women.
legal_issue: Whether sexual harassment at the workplace violates Articles 14, 15, and 21, and can the Supreme Court issue guidelines in the absence of legislation.
judgment: The Supreme Court held that sexual harassment violates gender equality and right to life with dignity. In the absence of a law, it formulated the 'Vishaka Guidelines' to prevent sexual harassment at workplaces.
ratio_decidendi: Gender equality and right to life with dignity require safe working environments. The Court can lay down guidelines under Article 141 to fill legislative vacuums.
legal_principle: Sexual harassment violates gender equality (Article 14) and dignity (Article 21). Court issued mandatory Vishaka Guidelines.

case_name: Samatha v. State of Andhra Pradesh
facts: The AP government leased out tribal land in Scheduled Areas to private mining companies, which was challenged by a tribal rights group.
legal_issue: Whether leasing tribal land to private mining companies violates the Fifth Schedule and state land transfer regulations.
judgment: The Supreme Court held that 'person' in land transfer regulations includes the State. Government cannot lease tribal land to non-tribals or private companies for mining; only state cooperatives or tribals can mine.
ratio_decidendi: Fifth Schedule aims to protect tribal autonomy and economic integrity. The state cannot transfer tribal land to non-tribals or private corporations.
legal_principle: Government cannot lease tribal land in Scheduled Areas to private companies for mining. Fifth Schedule protects tribal land.

case_name: Sunil Batra v. Delhi Administration
facts: A death row prisoner challenged his solitary confinement, raising concerns about torture and prison administration conditions.
legal_issue: Whether solitary confinement of a prisoner before final sentencing violates Articles 14, 19, and 21.
judgment: The Supreme Court held that solitary confinement cannot be imposed routinely. Prisoners do not lose their fundamental rights upon entering prison, and courts have a duty to protect their bodily integrity.
ratio_decidendi: Prisoners are human beings and retain all fundamental rights except those taken away by incarceration. Solitary confinement must follow strict judicial authorization.
legal_principle: Prisoners retain fundamental rights under Article 21. Solitary confinement must be strictly regulated and not applied routinely.

case_name: Hussainara Khatoon v. State of Bihar
facts: A news report revealed that thousands of undertrials in Bihar were languishing in jail for periods exceeding their maximum possible sentence.
legal_issue: Whether prolonged detention of undertrial prisoners violates the right to personal liberty under Article 21, and is free legal aid a fundamental right.
judgment: The Supreme Court held that a speedy trial is an essential part of Article 21. It ordered the immediate release of undertrials and held that free legal aid is a constitutional right.
ratio_decidendi: Speedy trial is a fundamental right implicit in the broad sweep of Article 21. Legal aid is a necessary ingredient of reasonable procedure.
legal_principle: Right to speedy trial is a fundamental right under Article 21. Free legal aid is mandatory for poor accused.

case_name: Mohini Jain v. State of Karnataka
facts: Private medical colleges in Karnataka charged high capitation fees from out-of-state students, effectively denying them admission.
legal_issue: Whether the right to education is a fundamental right under Article 21, and whether charging capitation fees is constitutional.
judgment: The Supreme Court held that the right to education is a fundamental right that flows directly from the right to life under Article 21, and struck down capitation fees.
ratio_decidendi: The right to life with dignity is impossible without education. State has an obligation to provide educational facilities.
legal_principle: Right to education is a fundamental right under Article 21. Capitation fees in professional colleges are unconstitutional.

case_name: T.M.A. Pai Foundation v. State of Karnataka
facts: Private unaided educational institutions challenged state regulations over their admission procedures and fee structures.
legal_issue: What is the extent of state control over admissions and fee structures in minority and non-minority private unaided educational institutions.
judgment: An 11-judge bench held that the right to establish and administer educational institutions is a fundamental right under Article 19(1)(g) for non-minorities and Article 30 for minorities. State regulation must be minimal for unaided colleges.
ratio_decidendi: Private institutions have autonomy to recruit staff, set fees, and admit students. State can regulate only to prevent commercialization and maintain academic standards.
legal_principle: Private unaided colleges have autonomy in admissions and fees. State regulation must be minimal and academic-focused.

case_name: P.A. Inamdar v. State of Maharashtra
facts: Private professional colleges challenged state laws imposing reservation quotas on their admissions.
legal_issue: Can the state impose reservation quotas (SC/ST/OBC) on private unaided educational institutions.
judgment: A 7-judge bench held that the state cannot force private unaided colleges (both minority and non-minority) to accept reservation quotas, as it violates their autonomy under Article 19(1)(g) and Article 30.
ratio_decidendi: The state cannot appropriate seats in private unaided colleges for reservation quotas. (This led to the 93rd Constitutional Amendment inserting Article 15(5)).
legal_principle: State cannot enforce reservation quotas in private unaided professional colleges.

case_name: I.R. Coelho v. State of Tamil Nadu
facts: Various land laws placed in the Ninth Schedule were challenged as violating fundamental rights.
legal_issue: Are laws placed in the Ninth Schedule after April 24, 1973, immune from judicial review, and how is basic structure applied.
judgment: A 9-judge bench held that Ninth Schedule laws are not immune. If a law added to the Ninth Schedule post-Kesavananda violates fundamental rights that form part of the basic structure, it will be struck down.
ratio_decidendi: The Ninth Schedule immunity is subject to the 'rights test' and 'impact test'. Laws must not damage the core identity or basic structure of the Constitution.
legal_principle: Ninth Schedule laws post-1973 are subject to judicial review. If they damage the basic structure, they are unconstitutional.

case_name: Aruna Ramchandra Shanbaug v. Union of India
facts: A petition was filed on behalf of Aruna Shanbaug, who was in a vegetative state for 37 years following an assault, seeking permission to stop feeding.
legal_issue: Whether Article 21 right to life includes the right to die, and whether passive euthanasia can be permitted.
judgment: The Supreme Court held that active euthanasia is illegal, but passive euthanasia (withdrawing life support) is permissible under strict guidelines, requiring High Court approval.
ratio_decidendi: The right to life includes the right to live with dignity, which extends to a dignified death. Passive euthanasia is permitted under judicial safeguards.
legal_principle: Passive euthanasia (withdrawing life support) is permissible for patients in a permanent vegetative state under strict High Court guidelines.

case_name: Lily Thomas v. Union of India
facts: A petition challenged Section 8(4) of the Representation of the People Act, which allowed convicted MPs/MLAs to retain their seats if they appealed within 3 months.
legal_issue: Whether Parliament has the power to defer the disqualification of convicted legislators under Section 8(4) of the RP Act.
judgment: The Supreme Court struck down Section 8(4) as unconstitutional. It held that disqualification takes effect immediately upon conviction, and Parliament cannot create a separate class of convicted legislators.
ratio_decidendi: Parliament cannot defer disqualification. Constitutional disqualification (Articles 102/191) operates immediately upon conviction.
legal_principle: Convicted MPs and MLAs are disqualified immediately upon conviction; Section 8(4) of the RPA is unconstitutional.

case_name: Shayara Bano v. Union of India
facts: A Muslim woman challenged the practice of instant triple talaq (talaq-e-biddat) as discriminatory and arbitrary.
legal_issue: Whether instant triple talaq is an essential religious practice protected under Article 25, and whether it violates Article 14.
judgment: A 5-judge bench, by a 3:2 majority, declared instant triple talaq unconstitutional. The majority held that it was arbitrary, violated Article 14, and was not an essential religious practice.
ratio_decidendi: Talaq-e-biddat is arbitrary and has no place in constitutional secularism. It violates Article 14 by permitting unilateral dissolution of marriage without reconciliation.
legal_principle: Instant triple talaq (talaq-e-biddat) is unconstitutional as it is manifestly arbitrary and violates Article 14.

case_name: Joseph Shine v. Union of India
facts: The petitioner challenged Section 497 of the IPC, which criminalized adultery but punished only men and treated the wife as the husband's property.
legal_issue: Whether Section 497 IPC violates the right to equality under Article 14 and the right to privacy/dignity under Article 21.
judgment: The Supreme Court struck down Section 497 IPC as unconstitutional. It held that the law was patriarchal, discriminated against women, and violated the right to sexual autonomy and privacy under Article 21.
ratio_decidendi: Adultery is a civil wrong but cannot be a criminal offense. Section 497 IPC treated women as chattels and violated bodily autonomy.
legal_principle: Criminal adultery law (Section 497 IPC) is unconstitutional as it violates gender equality and privacy.

case_name: Anuradha Bhasin v. Union of India
facts: Following the abrogation of Article 370 in Jammu & Kashmir, the government suspended internet services and restricted press movements indefinitely.
legal_issue: Whether indefinite internet shutdown violates freedom of speech and trade under Article 19, and can the state keep suspension orders secret.
judgment: The Supreme Court held that access to the internet is a fundamental right under Article 19(1)(a) and 19(1)(g). Indefinite shutdowns are illegal, and suspension orders must be published and subject to periodic reviews.
ratio_decidendi: Freedom of speech and trade online is protected. Internet shutdowns must be temporary, proportionate, and subject to judicial review.
legal_principle: Internet access is a fundamental right. Indefinite internet shutdowns violate Article 19 and must pass the proportionality test.

case_name: Association for Democratic Reforms v. Union of India
facts: The Electoral Bonds Scheme, which allowed anonymous financial donations to political parties, was challenged by civic groups.
legal_issue: Whether anonymous political donations under the Electoral Bonds Scheme violate the voter's right to information under Article 19(1)(a).
judgment: A 5-judge bench unanimously struck down the Electoral Bonds Scheme. It held that the scheme violated the voter's right to information and was not a proportionate restriction to curb black money.
ratio_decidendi: Voters have a right to know the funding sources of political parties. Anonymity in donations violates Article 19(1)(a) and distorts free and fair elections.
legal_principle: Electoral Bonds Scheme is unconstitutional as it violates the voter's right to information under Article 19(1)(a).
